title: Is the zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_17 × Z_17 × Z_2) prime?
generalization_signal: Skeptical verification confirms that the six-class ring-level reduction survives unchanged at p = 17, but the old {2,3}-smooth reservoir truly fails by count: only 28 nontrivial {2,3}-smooth labels lie up to 321, short of the needed 32 for A ∪ E. The instance is still prime by a repaired fixed-support-set witness with S = {2,3,5}, giving 55 nontrivial S-smooth labels and 85 usable labels in {2,...,321} coprime to 30.
strongest_honest_claim: An explicit prime-labeling witness for Γ(Z_17 × Z_17 × Z_2) survives skeptical verification. The campaign-relevant gain is not just another exact feeder: p = 17 is the first honest failure point of the old {2,3}-smooth arithmetic template on the six-class line, and the same structural reduction still closes after a minimal repair to a fixed support-prime set S = {2,3,5}. This supports a real family sufficient-condition slice, but it is still only a verified feeder and not a Lean-closed theorem.
candidate_theorem_slice: After fixing the singleton class C of Γ(Z_p × Z_p × Z_2) to label 1, a sufficient-condition slice is visible: if {1, ..., p^2 + 2p - 2} contains 2(p - 1) labels for A ∪ E supported on a fixed finite prime set S and another 2(p - 1) labels for B ∪ F coprime to every prime in S, then the graph is prime. The verified p = 17 feeder realizes this with S = {2,3,5}.
next_action: Run generalize on the repaired six-class support-template with S = {2,3,5}, then test whether the same arithmetic idea survives at Γ(Z_19 × Z_19 × Z_2).
